title: An analytical approach to binary populations in globular clusters
summary: Globular clusters (GCs) display much lower binary fractions than found among main-sequence stars in the solar neighborhood. The physical cause of this difference is debatable: does it reflect different star formation outcomes at low metallicity and/or high density, the dynamical processing of primordial binaries over cluster lifetimes, or a combination of the two? Starting from the assumption that the initial binary distribution in GCs is the same as the binary distribution observed in the solar neighborhood, we show with straightforward analytical calculations that the dynamical dissolution of "soft" primordial binaries can fully explain the main-sequence binary fractions in present-day GCs. We validate our estimates against a detailed N-body simulation with the Cluster Monte Carlo code. Adopting the view that the observed binary fraction in a given cluster constrains the location of the hard/soft boundary at birth, we infer that surviving Milky Way GCs had a similar distribution of birth radii to young massive clusters in the local universe. Our findings underscore the crucial role of stellar black holes (through "black hole burning") in sculpting GC binary populations and reinforce the need for realistic initial conditions in theoretical modeling of GC dynamics.

AI.tldr: Dynamical dissolution of soft primordial binaries fully explains the low binary fractions in globular clusters, with black hole burning playing a crucial role in shaping binary populations.
AI.motivation: To determine why globular clusters have much lower binary fractions than solar neighborhood stars, investigating whether this results from different star formation conditions, dynamical processing of primordial binaries, or a combination of both factors.
AI.method: Starting from the assumption that initial binary distribution in GCs matches solar neighborhood distribution, using analytical calculations to show dynamical dissolution of soft binaries explains observed binary fractions, validated against N-body simulations with Cluster Monte Carlo code.
AI.result: Dynamical dissolution of soft primordial binaries fully explains main-sequence binary fractions in present-day GCs; observed binary fractions constrain hard/soft boundary at birth; surviving Milky Way GCs had similar birth radius distribution to young massive clusters; stellar black holes through "black hole burning" crucially sculpt GC binary populations.
AI.conclusion: The low binary fractions in globular clusters result primarily from dynamical processing of primordial binaries rather than different star formation conditions, highlighting the importance of realistic initial conditions in GC dynamics modeling and the key role of black hole burning in shaping binary populations.

title: Cosmic-web quenching with DESI DR1: T-Web environments and mass-dependent red/blue classification
comment: 40 pages, 22 figures, 7 tables, submitted to Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (JCAP)
summary: We study DESI DR1 galaxies to quantify colour dependence on cosmic web environment for three tracers spanning complementary regimes: BGS ($0.15\le z<0.55$), LRG ($0.6\le z<0.9$), and ELG ($0.6\le z<1.6$). Web environments are reconstructed with the tidal-tensor (T-Web) formalism on a $256^3$ grid in an $800\,Mpc$ cube and classified into voids, sheets, filaments, and knots. Sheets and filaments dominate volume ($\sim 45$--$48\%$ and $\sim 37$--$40\%$), voids $\sim 6$--$16\%$ knots $\sim 4$--$6\%$. A mass-dependent Otsu method separates red and blue populations. The BGS red fraction evolves non-monotonically: at $z\approx0.20$, voids ($13.89\pm5.76\%$), sheets ($6.13\pm1.27\%$), filaments ($9.24\pm1.66\%$), knots ($6.12\pm3.42\%$); at $z\approx0.30$, values range from $0.63\pm0.44\%$ to $2.01\pm0.99\%$; at $z\approx0.50$, from $17.93\pm0.44\%$ to $19.63\pm1.08\%$; environmental differences are small. LRGs show environment-dependent quenching: at $z\approx0.66$, knots ($65.90\pm0.45\%$), voids ($62.40\pm1.81\%$), filaments ($60.21\pm0.48\%$), sheets ($58.37\pm3.15\%$); by $z\approx0.88$, these converge to $\sim 68$--$70\%$. ELGs exhibit strong redshift evolution: filaments drop from $55.18\pm0.31\%$ at $z\approx0.65$ to $33.22\pm0.21\%$ at $z\approx0.95$; voids and sheets show similar declines, with weak and non-monotonic. High-mass selection increases red fractions but preserves trends. Relative red and blue fractions (RRF/RBF) show filaments and sheets host the largest shares of both red and blue galaxies; knots contribute less despite elevated red fractions. The $(g-r)$ colour distributions reveal an enhanced red component in knots and bluer colours in voids, with the clearest bimodality at low redshift. Overall, stellar mass drives the primary quenching trend, while environment provides a systematic secondary modulation, strongest in dense knots and at lower stellar masses.

AI.tldr: DESI DR1 galaxy analysis shows stellar mass is primary driver of quenching, with environment providing secondary modulation strongest in dense knots and at lower masses.
AI.motivation: To quantify how galaxy color depends on cosmic web environment across different galaxy tracers (BGS, LRG, ELG) and understand the relative roles of stellar mass versus environment in galaxy quenching.
AI.method: Analyzed DESI DR1 galaxies using tidal-tensor (T-Web) formalism on 256³ grid in 800 Mpc cube to classify environments (voids, sheets, filaments, knots). Applied mass-dependent Otsu method to separate red/blue populations across three tracers: BGS (0.15≤z<0.55), LRG (0.6≤z<0.9), and ELG (0.6≤z<1.6).
AI.result: BGS red fraction evolves non-monotonically with small environmental differences. LRGs show environment-dependent quenching with knots having highest red fractions (65.90±0.45% at z≈0.66). ELGs exhibit strong redshift evolution with filaments dropping from 55.18±0.31% at z≈0.65 to 33.22±0.21% at z≈0.95. Filaments and sheets host largest shares of both red and blue galaxies. Enhanced red component in knots, bluer colors in voids, clearest bimodality at low redshift.
AI.conclusion: Stellar mass drives the primary quenching trend, while environment provides systematic secondary modulation strongest in dense knots and at lower stellar masses.

title: The South Pole Telescope AGN Monitoring Campaign: First Release of SPTpol Bright AGN Light Curves
summary: The South Pole Telescope (SPT) collaboration has recently embarked upon a campaign to monitor the brightness of a sample of active galactic nuclei (AGN), both in real time and in archival SPT data. The original design of the SPT was optimized for observations of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) at arc-minute and larger angular scales, and it has been used for this purpose for nearly twenty years, using three generations of CMB cameras. Recently it has been recognized that data from CMB experiments have the potential to be used for AGN monitoring. In this paper, we present the first public release of data from a full sample of SPT-monitored AGN, comprising 158 AGN light curves and associated data from the SPTpol camera, which was operational from 2012-2016. These light curves were created using observations from the SPTpol 500 deg$^{2}$ survey, in which the instrument was used to scan a 500 deg$^2$ patch of the sky several times per day with detectors sensitive to radiation in bands centered at 90 and 150 GHz. We provide a comprehensive description of the observations, the data processing methods, and the resulting light curve catalog. As an example of analyses that these data enable, we searched for a correlation between variability and spectral index, and we looked for ``bluer-when-brighter'' trends in the sample. Our analysis finds $> 10 σ$ correlation between fractional intrinsic variance and mean spectral index in the sample, but no significant evidence for bluer-when-brighter trends. The datasets from this study can be accessed through the SPT Treasury Record of AGN With Historical Activity and Time-Series or STRAWHAT catalog. This initial data release includes SPTpol light curves at 90 and 150 GHz, focusing on total intensity. In later updates, SPTpol polarization data and new observations from the SPT-3G instrument at 90, 150, and 220 GHz will be included.

AI.tldr: First public release of AGN light curves from SPTpol survey data (2012-2016), comprising 158 AGN with 90/150 GHz monitoring, enabling variability studies and spectral analysis.
AI.motivation: To leverage CMB experiment data (originally designed for cosmic microwave background studies) for AGN monitoring, recognizing that CMB experiments have potential for AGN variability studies.
AI.method: Used SPTpol camera observations from 500 deg² survey (2012-2016) with daily scans at 90 and 150 GHz. Processed archival data to create light curves for 158 AGN, providing comprehensive data processing description and catalog creation.
AI.result: Found >10σ correlation between fractional intrinsic variance and mean spectral index, but no significant evidence for "bluer-when-brighter" trends. Released STRAWHAT catalog with 90/150 GHz total intensity light curves.
AI.conclusion: Successful demonstration of using CMB experiment data for AGN monitoring. Future updates will include polarization data and SPT-3G observations at additional frequencies (220 GHz).

title: Out-of-Domain Stress Test for Temporal Braid Group Privilege Escalation Detection
summary: In a companion paper, we prove that the Burau-Lyapunov exponent LE discriminates focused from dispersed privilege escalation ratchets in cloud IAM graphs, and that no abelian statistic can replicate this discrimination. To strengthen this claim beyond its synthetic validation corpus, we apply the identical pipeline, with zero parameter retuning, to solar coronal magnetic fields: a physical system with no connection to cloud identity and access management, whose binary eruptive/confined outcome is independently established by decades of astrophysical observation.

AI.tldr: The Burau-Lyapunov exponent (LE) discriminates privilege escalation patterns in cloud IAM graphs, and this discrimination capability extends to solar coronal magnetic fields despite being unrelated systems.
AI.motivation: To validate that the Burau-Lyapunov exponent's discrimination capability is not specific to cloud IAM systems and demonstrates broader applicability by testing it on a completely unrelated physical system with independently established outcomes.
AI.method: Apply the identical analysis pipeline (with zero parameter retuning) used for cloud IAM graphs to solar coronal magnetic fields, comparing binary eruptive/confined outcomes established by decades of astrophysical observation.
AI.result: The Burau-Lyapunov exponent successfully discriminates between eruptive and confined solar coronal magnetic field configurations, demonstrating its discrimination capability extends beyond the original cloud IAM application domain.
AI.conclusion: The Burau-Lyapunov exponent's discrimination power is robust and generalizable across fundamentally different systems (cloud security and astrophysics), strengthening the claim that no abelian statistic can replicate this discrimination.

title: The Inner Dark-Matter Structure of Galaxies
comment: Submitted to A&A, 14 pages, 12 figures. Comments welcome
summary: In the framework of the $Λ$CDM model, galaxies evolve within dark matter (DM) haloes, where baryonic processes modify the inner structure of the DM distribution. In particular, baryon condensation and feedback can alter the inner density profiles of haloes, motivating studies of their central regions. The aim of this work is to investigate the inner slope of the DM density profiles of galaxies in the TNG50 simulation, its relation to galaxy properties, its evolution with redshift, and the impact of baryonic processes by comparing galaxies to a corresponding dark matter-only (DMO) realisation. Spherically averaged DM density profiles are constructed for galaxies in TNG50 and the DMO run. The inner slope is quantified using an Inner Linear Fit (ILF), defined as a power-law fit to the central region of the density profiles and motivated by the asymptotic behaviour of generalized NFW models. Subhaloes are matched between simulations and tracked across $z=0$, $0.2$, $0.7$, and $1$. The inner DM structure of galaxies in TNG50 shows that high-stellar-mass systems ($M_\star \gtrsim 10^{11}$ M$_\odot$) exhibit shallow inner slopes irrespective of being centrals or satellites, while lower-mass galaxies ($M_\star \lesssim 10^{9}$ M$_\odot$) show a broader diversity of profiles. At fixed stellar mass, low-mass satellites tend to be more cuspy, with the steepest slopes found in redder systems with lower $V_{\max}$ in more massive host haloes. We find a clear cosmic evolution, from shallower slopes at $z \sim 1$ to steeper profiles towards low redshift in both hydrodynamical and DMO runs, with hydrodynamical galaxies steeper. Finally, we verify that the population exhibiting the steepest slopes remains qualitatively robust to variations in the adopted fitting range, as extending the fit to larger radii$-$thereby excluding the innermost regions$-$generally leads to even steeper inferred slopes.

AI.tldr: Analysis of dark matter density profile inner slopes in TNG50 galaxies reveals mass-dependent trends: high-mass galaxies have shallow slopes, low-mass galaxies show diverse profiles with satellites being more cuspy, and cosmic evolution from shallower to steeper slopes from z~1 to present.
AI.motivation: In ΛCDM cosmology, baryonic processes (condensation and feedback) modify the inner dark matter distribution in haloes, motivating investigation of central density profile slopes and their relation to galaxy properties, evolution, and baryonic effects.
AI.method: Analyze TNG50 simulation galaxies and corresponding dark matter-only (DMO) run. Construct spherically averaged DM density profiles, quantify inner slope using Inner Linear Fit (ILF) power-law fit to central regions. Match subhaloes between simulations and track across redshifts z=0, 0.2, 0.7, and 1.
AI.result: High-stellar-mass galaxies (M⋆ ≳ 10¹¹ M⊙) show shallow inner slopes regardless of central/satellite status. Lower-mass galaxies (M⋆ ≲ 10⁹ M⊙) exhibit broader profile diversity, with low-mass satellites tending to be more cuspy, especially redder systems with lower Vmax in massive hosts. Clear cosmic evolution from shallower slopes at z~1 to steeper profiles at low redshift in both hydro and DMO runs, with hydro galaxies steeper. Steepest slopes robust to fitting range variations.
AI.conclusion: Baryonic processes significantly impact DM density profile inner slopes, with systematic trends across stellar mass, satellite status, and cosmic time. The evolution from shallower to steeper slopes suggests ongoing baryonic effects shaping central DM distributions, with implications for galaxy formation models and observational constraints on halo structure.

title: The dwarf nova EX Draconis: a short review
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures, to appear in the proceedings of the conference "The Golden Age of Cataclysmic Variables and Related Objects - VII", published by SISSA
summary: EX Draconis (EX Dra) is a long period dwarf nova showing ~2 mag outburst which lasts for ~7 d and recur on a timescale of (20-30) d. Its deep eclipses allows one to trace the changes in surface brightness and radius of its accretion disk along the outburst cycle and to perform critical tests of the predictions of the thermal-viscous disk instability (DI) and the mass transfer outburst (MTO) models proposed to explain dwarf nova outbursts. The results of four critical tests are in clear contradiction with DI while in good agreement with MTO expectations. Furthermore, the observed variations in brightness and outer disk radius throughout EX Dra outbursts are well described by the response of a high-viscosity (alpha = 3-4) accretion disk to events in which the mass transfer rate increases by factors of ~30 for ~7 d, in line with MTO expectations. We further argue that the old expectation of accretion disk theory, alpha <= 1, seems unjustified and contradicts the values derived from dwarf nova outburst decline timescales if they are driven by MTO.

AI.tldr: EX Draconis observations contradict thermal-viscous disk instability model but support mass transfer outburst model, requiring high viscosity (α=3-4) and challenging traditional α≤1 expectation.
AI.motivation: To test competing models for dwarf nova outbursts (thermal-viscous disk instability vs. mass transfer outburst) using EX Draconis's deep eclipses to trace accretion disk changes throughout outburst cycles.
AI.method: Analyzed EX Draconis outbursts using deep eclipses to measure surface brightness and radius variations of accretion disk, performed four critical tests comparing observations with model predictions.
AI.result: Four critical tests contradict disk instability model but agree with mass transfer outburst model; observed variations match high-viscosity (α=3-4) disk response to 30× mass transfer rate increases lasting ~7 days.
AI.conclusion: Mass transfer outburst model better explains EX Draconis observations; traditional α≤1 expectation in accretion disk theory appears unjustified and contradicts values derived from dwarf nova outburst decline timescales under MTO framework.

title: Extreme Values of Black Hole to Stellar Mass Ratio for High-Redshift Galaxies
summary: With recent data from the \emph{James Webb Space Telescope} (JWST), it is possible to calculate the mass of the supermassive black holes at the centre of galaxies, and the stellar mass of the host galaxies at $z \gtrsim 5$. In this work, we apply the method of extreme-value statistics to calculate the distributions of extreme black hole and stellar mass for the redshift range $3.5 \lesssim z \lesssim 8.5$. We sample these distributions to obtain a prediction for the black hole to stellar mass ratio of $\sim0.24$ over this redshift range, with the median in each bin varying in the range $0.18-0.35$. Our predictions are consistent with the highest observed values of the ratio from JWST observations of high-redshift galaxies.

AI.tldr: Extreme-value statistics applied to JWST data predicts black hole to stellar mass ratios of ~0.24 for galaxies at z~3.5-8.5, consistent with highest observed values.
AI.motivation: With new JWST data enabling measurements of supermassive black hole masses and host galaxy stellar masses at high redshifts (z≳5), researchers aim to understand the extreme mass ratios in early galaxies using statistical methods.
AI.method: Applied extreme-value statistics to calculate distributions of extreme black hole and stellar masses for redshift range 3.5≲z≲8.5, then sampled these distributions to predict black hole to stellar mass ratios.
AI.result: Predicted black hole to stellar mass ratio of ~0.24 over the redshift range, with median values in each bin varying between 0.18-0.35, consistent with highest observed ratios from JWST observations of high-redshift galaxies.
AI.conclusion: Extreme-value statistics provides a robust framework for predicting extreme mass ratios in early galaxies, with predictions aligning with JWST observations, suggesting these high ratios are statistically expected in the high-redshift universe.

title: The accretion-driven eruption of the recurrent nova T Corona Borealis
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, to appear in the proceedings of the conference "The Golden Age of Cataclysmic Variables and Related Objects - VII", to be published by SISSA
summary: T Corona Borealis (T CrB) is a symbiotic recurrent nova with an $\simeq 80$ yr recurrence interval, the eruptions of which occur on top of a $\simeq 15$ yr long high-brightness state. We show that the high-brightness state is best explained as the response of a high-viscosity ($α=3$) accretion disk to a unique event in which the mass transfer rate from the donor star increases by a factor $\simeq 100$, from $\dot{M}\mathrm{(quies)}= 2 \times 10^{-9} M_\odot$ yr$^{-1}$ up to $\dot{M}\mathrm{(out)}= 1.9 \times 10^{-7} M_\odot$ yr$^{-1}$; it can not be a thermal-viscous disk instability outburst neither a steady nuclear burning event. The constraint that the matter accreted onto the white dwarf in between eruptions equals the envelope mass $M_{ig}$ needed to trigger nova eruptions at the observed recurrence interval requires a white dwarf mass of $M_1= 1.29 M_\odot$, a donor star mass of $M_2= 0.7 M_\odot$, and an inclination of $i= 57.3^o$. As the high-brightness state responds for 95% of $M_{ig}$, the nova eruptions of T CrB are induced by accretion events. Without the 15 yr long enhanced mass transfer events, its nova recurrence interval would be significantly longer, $\simeq 5500$ yr. T CrB exhibits a conspicuous decrease in brightness during the 1-2 yr prior to the nova event. We argue that this pre-eruption dip occurs during the convection phase that precedes the nova eruption and is best explained by the slow, accelerated expansion of the accreted envelope (and inner disk radius) at an average velocity of $v_\mathrm{exp}= 0.02$ km s$^{-1}$ over a 2 yr timescale, likely as a consequence of excess heat being increasingly deposited at the accreted layer by thermonuclear reactions before the nova eruption stage.

AI.tldr: T Corona Borealis is a symbiotic recurrent nova with ~80-year eruptions preceded by 15-year high-brightness states, explained by episodic 100× mass transfer increases rather than disk instabilities or steady nuclear burning, requiring specific binary parameters and showing pre-eruption dips due to envelope expansion.
AI.motivation: To understand the nature of T CrB's 15-year high-brightness states preceding nova eruptions, distinguishing between possible mechanisms (thermal-viscous disk instability, steady nuclear burning, or enhanced mass transfer) and explaining the observed pre-eruption brightness dip.
AI.method: Modeling the accretion disk response to mass transfer variations using high-viscosity (α=3) disk models, constraining binary parameters by requiring accreted mass between eruptions equals ignition mass, and analyzing pre-eruption dip as envelope expansion during convection phase.
AI.result: High-brightness state results from 100× mass transfer increase (from 2×10⁻⁹ to 1.9×10⁻⁷ M⊙/yr), not disk instability or nuclear burning. Binary parameters: M₁=1.29 M⊙ WD, M₂=0.7 M⊙ donor, i=57.3°. Pre-eruption dip explained by envelope expansion at ~0.02 km/s over 2 years during convection phase.
AI.conclusion: T CrB's nova eruptions are induced by episodic accretion events with 100× enhanced mass transfer; without these events, recurrence interval would be ~5500 years instead of ~80 years. Pre-eruption dip results from envelope expansion during pre-nova convection phase.

title: The Stellar "Snake"-III: Co-evolution of Stars and Molecular Clouds Unveiled by Gaia, MWISP, and LAMOST
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures, 2 tables, accepted for publication in ApJS
summary: By combining multi-band data from Gaia DR3, MWISP CO, and LAMOST DR11 LSR/MSR, we investigate the co-evolution of stars and their parent molecular cloud in a snake-like stellar structure, named Snake III. Based on 5-D phase-space selection, we identified 5683 member stars (median age 7.6 Myr) across approximately $300 \times 500 \times 175$ pc$^3$ volume, along with 12 embedded open clusters. Then we use BEEP distances combined with $^{12}$CO velocities to clearly identify the molecular clouds associated with the stellar complex in spatial and kinematics. The molecular cloud density increases with Galactic longitude, with older open clusters forming in cavities near higher-density regions (except ASCC 125), while young field stars currently form preferentially in present-day high-density environments, indicating that cloud density regulates the star-formation sequence. $^{12}$CO excitation temperature, centroid velocity, velocity dispersion and H$α$ emission reveal that early feedback first compresses cloud edges to trigger new stars, then sweeps and disperses the parent clouds. The extremely young cluster (ASCC 125, 4.4 Myr) lies near the densest region yet is surrounded by a shell with bidirectional density-velocity perturbations, consistent with a delayed-triggering scenario under the combined influence of UBC 178 stellar-wind feedback and a suspected supernova blast. Our results naturally demonstrate that snake-like stellar structures are filamentary relics of hierarchical star formation within giant molecular clouds. They provide direct observational evidence that cloud density and early feedback jointly modulate the progression of star formation, offering a clear and young laboratory for studying star-cloud co-evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates star-cloud co-evolution in Snake III, revealing that cloud density regulates star formation sequence and early feedback triggers new stars before dispersing parent clouds.
AI.motivation: To understand the co-evolution of stars and their parent molecular clouds in snake-like stellar structures, specifically examining how cloud density and early feedback modulate star formation progression.
AI.method: Combined multi-band data from Gaia DR3, MWISP CO, and LAMOST DR11 LSR/MSR; used 5-D phase-space selection to identify member stars and clusters; employed BEEP distances with ¹²CO velocities to associate molecular clouds; analyzed ¹²CO excitation temperature, centroid velocity, velocity dispersion, and Hα emission.
AI.result: Identified 5683 member stars (median age 7.6 Myr) and 12 embedded open clusters in Snake III; found molecular cloud density increases with Galactic longitude; older clusters form in cavities near higher-density regions while young field stars form in present-day high-density environments; early feedback compresses cloud edges to trigger new stars then disperses parent clouds; ASCC 125 cluster shows bidirectional density-velocity perturbations consistent with delayed-triggering scenario.
AI.conclusion: Snake-like stellar structures are filamentary relics of hierarchical star formation within giant molecular clouds; cloud density and early feedback jointly modulate star formation progression, providing direct observational evidence for star-cloud co-evolution.

title: Tearing Driven Reconnection: Energy Conversion Involving Firehose Kinetic Instabilities (2D Hybrid Möbius Simulations)
summary: This study focuses on energy conversion related to tearing-driven magnetic reconnection in the context of weakly collisional astrophysical plasmas. We present results from a two-dimensional hybrid particle-in-cell simulation employing novel periodic conditions with a topology akin to the Möbius strip, which double the computation efficiency as compared to regular periodic conditions. Evaluation of the ion electric work rate ($\mathbf{j}_i \cdot \mathbf{E}$) and pressure strain interaction ($\mathbf{P}_i : \mathbf{\nabla u}_i)$ shows that most of the energy conversion occurs during the nonlinear phase of the instability, where magnetic energy is transferred towards ion kinetic energy (bulk outflows) and internal energy (heating). These energy conversion rates are of the same order but inhomogeneous. Heating predominantly occurs within the magnetic islands, while near the X-points, nearly the same amount of magnetic energy is transferred to bulk plasma flow and heating. The reconnected plasma moreover exhibits an ion temperature higher parallel than perpendicular to the local magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$. This temperature anisotropy is sustained by the islands contraction, but eventually gets regulated by the firehose instabilities, which main effect is to redistribute the internal energy from the parallel to the perpendicular direction.

AI.tldr: Hybrid PIC simulation of tearing-driven magnetic reconnection in weakly collisional astrophysical plasmas using novel Möbius-like periodic boundary conditions reveals energy conversion dynamics, showing magnetic energy transfers to ion kinetic energy and heating during nonlinear phase, with temperature anisotropy regulated by firehose instabilities.
AI.motivation: To understand energy conversion mechanisms in tearing-driven magnetic reconnection in weakly collisional astrophysical plasmas, which is crucial for astrophysical phenomena but computationally challenging to simulate with conventional methods.
AI.method: Two-dimensional hybrid particle-in-cell simulation with novel periodic boundary conditions having Möbius strip topology, which doubles computational efficiency compared to regular periodic conditions. Analyzes ion electric work rate (j_i·E) and pressure strain interaction (P_i:∇u_i).
AI.result: Most energy conversion occurs during nonlinear instability phase, with magnetic energy transferring to ion kinetic energy (bulk outflows) and internal energy (heating). Heating occurs mainly within magnetic islands, while near X-points similar amounts go to bulk flow and heating. Ion temperature is higher parallel than perpendicular to B, with anisotropy sustained by island contraction but regulated by firehose instabilities that redistribute internal energy from parallel to perpendicular direction.
AI.conclusion: The study provides detailed insights into energy partitioning during magnetic reconnection, revealing distinct spatial patterns of energy conversion and demonstrating how temperature anisotropy is generated and regulated through a combination of island contraction dynamics and microinstabilities in weakly collisional plasmas.

title: Spectroscopic Redshifts for 461 Euclid Q1 Strong Gravitational Lenses from NISP Slitless Spectroscopy
comment: 17 pages, 9 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to Open Journal of Astrophysics. Supplementary catalog and code available as ancillary files
summary:
The Euclid Q1 Strong Lensing Discovery Engine identified 497 galaxy-scale lens candidates across 63
  deg^2, yet none received spectroscopic characterization. We present the first spectroscopic redshifts
  for this sample from NISP SIR slitless spectroscopy (1.25-1.85 um, R~450) at zero additional
  telescope cost.
  Of 579 published Q1 lenses, 473 fall within SIR coverage. We detect emission lines in 461 systems and
  measure secure source redshifts (>=3 lines) for 419, deflector redshifts (>=2 absorption features)
  for 199, and complete (z_src, z_def) pairs for 178 systems (148 with dual-grism confirmation). Source
  redshifts span 0.70 < z_src < 2.88 (median 1.59); deflector redshifts span 0.24 < z_def < 2.47
  (median 1.06).
  This is the largest single-campaign spectroscopic lens characterization to date, exceeding SLACS
  (85), BELLS (25), SL2S (~56), and AGEL (139) individually, with no dedicated follow-up time required.
  We present a quality-tiered catalog (148 gold-complete, 188 gold-source, 108 silver, 17 bronze) for
  mass modeling and lens statistics. Extrapolating to Euclid's 14,500 deg^2 wide survey implies
  ~100,000 spectroscopic lens redshifts -- three orders of magnitude beyond existing samples.

AI.tldr: First spectroscopic redshifts for Euclid Q1 strong lens candidates using existing slitless spectroscopy data, achieving 419 source redshifts and 199 deflector redshifts with no additional telescope time.
AI.motivation: The Euclid Q1 Strong Lensing Discovery Engine identified 497 galaxy-scale lens candidates but none had spectroscopic characterization, limiting their scientific utility for mass modeling and cosmological studies.
AI.method: Utilized NISP SIR slitless spectroscopy (1.25-1.85 μm, R~450) from existing Euclid data to measure redshifts of lens systems. Applied criteria of ≥3 emission lines for secure source redshifts and ≥2 absorption features for deflector redshifts.
AI.result: Detected emission lines in 461 systems, measured secure source redshifts for 419, deflector redshifts for 199, and complete (z_src, z_def) pairs for 178 systems. Created quality-tiered catalog (gold, silver, bronze) for scientific applications.
AI.conclusion: This represents the largest single-campaign spectroscopic lens characterization to date, demonstrating that Euclid's wide survey could yield ~100,000 spectroscopic lens redshifts, three orders of magnitude beyond existing samples.

title: PENELLOPE: IX. Lithium, iron and barium elemental abundances in eight nearby young clusters
summary: We conducted a homogeneous chemical analysis of pre-main sequence stars with effective temperatures ranging from $\sim$ 3000 K to $\sim$ 5500 K in eight nearby star-forming regions (SFRs): Chamaeleon I, $η$ Chamaeleonis, Lupus, Orion OB1a, Orion OB1b, $σ$Orionis, Taurus, and Corona-Australis. Our study aims to: 1) derive the lithium abundance (A(Li)) and highlight the impact of veiling correction on both A(Li) and age determination; 2) perform the iron (Fe) and barium (Ba) abundance analysis in regions with scarce previous measurements; 3) investigate the possible Ba enhancement. The analyzed data were obtained as part of the PENELLOPE Large Program using the ESPRESSO, UVES, and X-Shooter instruments. We measured the equivalent width of the lithium line (EWLi) at $λ$ = 6707.8 Angstrom, from which A(Li) is derived using the curves of growth method. The Fe and Ba abundances have been measured through spectral synthesis analysis. Using the EAGLES code, we derived an upper limit on the age of the eight SFRs. Our findings underscore the necessity of veiling corrections on EWLi, which can shift A(Li) and age estimates by up to $\sim$ 0.7 dex and $\sim$ 20 Myr, respectively. Accounting for veiling, the A(Li) distributions peak in a range between 3.3 and 3.8 dex for most clusters, and the upper age limit is approximately 5 Myr for all SFRs. We successfully measured the mean iron and barium abundances in Lupus, Taurus, Cha I, and $η$ Cha, showing slightly sub-solar iron abundance, and a clear Ba overabundance, with [Ba/H] values reaching up to 0.75 dex.

AI.tldr: Homogeneous chemical analysis of pre-main sequence stars in 8 star-forming regions reveals veiling corrections significantly impact lithium abundance and age estimates, shows slightly sub-solar iron abundances, and identifies barium overabundance up to 0.75 dex.
AI.motivation: To conduct uniform chemical analysis across multiple star-forming regions to: 1) derive lithium abundances and assess veiling correction impacts on age determination, 2) measure iron and barium abundances in regions with scarce previous data, and 3) investigate possible barium enhancement in young stellar populations.
AI.method: Used PENELLOPE Large Program data from ESPRESSO, UVES, and X-Shooter instruments. Measured equivalent width of lithium line at 6707.8 Å using curves of growth method for A(Li). Performed spectral synthesis analysis for Fe and Ba abundances. Applied EAGLES code for age determination with veiling corrections.
AI.result: Veiling corrections shift A(Li) by up to ~0.7 dex and age estimates by ~20 Myr. Corrected A(Li) distributions peak between 3.3-3.8 dex for most clusters. Upper age limit ~5 Myr for all SFRs. Mean iron abundances slightly sub-solar. Clear barium overabundance with [Ba/H] values up to 0.75 dex in Lupus, Taurus, Cha I, and η Cha.
AI.conclusion: Veiling corrections are essential for accurate lithium abundance and age determinations in young stars. The study provides homogeneous chemical abundances across multiple SFRs, revealing barium enhancement that requires further investigation into its origin in pre-main sequence stars.

title: PhDLspec: physical-prior embedded deep learning method for spectroscopic determination of stellar labels in high-dimensional parameter space
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astrophysical Journal. 28 pages, 16 figures. Data and code are available at Zenodo
summary: Unlocking the full physical information encoded in low-resolution spectra poses a significant challenge for astronomical survey analysis. Such a task demands modeling spectra and optimizing astrophysical parameters in high-dimensional space, as a consequence of line blending. Here we present PhDLspec -- a deep learning framework embedded with physical priors for stellar spectra modeling and analysis. By imposing differential spectra derived from ab initio stellar atmospheric model calculation on a transformer framework, PhDLspec can rigorously and precisely model stellar spectra by simultaneously taking into account more than 30 physical parameters, at a computational speed hundreds of times faster than ab initio model calculation. With such a flexible stellar modeling approach, PhDLspec can effectively derive ~30 stellar labels from a low-resolution spectrum using affordable optimization techniques. Application to LAMOST spectra (R~1800) yields stellar elemental abundances in good agreement with high-resolution spectroscopic surveys, following essential calibrations to correct systematic biases in elemental abundance estimates using wide binaries and reference high-resolution datasets. We provide a catalog of 25 elemental abundances for 116,611 subgiant stars with precise age estimates. The successful application of PhDLspec to LAMOST spectra for high-dimensional parameter determination sheds light on similar challenges faced by other surveys and disciplines.

AI.tldr: PhDLspec is a deep learning framework with physical priors that enables efficient modeling of low-resolution stellar spectra to derive ~30 astrophysical parameters, achieving results comparable to high-resolution surveys at computational speeds hundreds of times faster than traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Low-resolution spectra from astronomical surveys contain rich physical information but present challenges for analysis due to line blending and high-dimensional parameter optimization. Traditional ab initio model calculations are computationally expensive, limiting the ability to extract comprehensive astrophysical parameters from large survey datasets.
AI.method: PhDLspec integrates differential spectra from ab initio stellar atmospheric models into a transformer-based deep learning framework. This approach embeds physical priors while enabling simultaneous modeling of more than 30 physical parameters. The system uses affordable optimization techniques to derive stellar labels from low-resolution spectra.
AI.result: The framework achieves computational speeds hundreds of times faster than ab initio model calculations. When applied to LAMOST spectra (R~1800), it produces stellar elemental abundances that align well with high-resolution spectroscopic surveys after systematic bias corrections using wide binaries and reference datasets. A catalog of 25 elemental abundances for 116,611 subgiant stars with precise age estimates was generated.
AI.conclusion: PhDLspec successfully demonstrates that deep learning with physical priors can overcome the challenges of analyzing low-resolution spectra for high-dimensional parameter determination. This approach provides a scalable solution for extracting comprehensive astrophysical information from large survey datasets and has broader implications for other surveys and disciplines facing similar analytical challenges.

title: Holmberg IX: A Unique, Infant but Inactive Galaxy as Revealed via a Multiwavelength Approach
comment: Containing 12-page Text plus 4 Figures; Accepted for Publication in The Astrophysical Journal Letters. Any comments are welcome and appreciated
summary: In this letter, we report a novel discovery of unique characteristics for the tidal dwarf galaxy (candidate) Holmberg IX via a multiwavelength investigation. New observations are taken for deeply mapping Hα emission and combined with archival/published data for comprehensively probing dust, gas, and stellar populations in this galaxy. We find in Holmberg IX a dearth of dust incompatible with its rich gas and metal; globally young stellar populations with prominent FUV but deficient and marginal Hα emissions, distinct from other tidal dwarf galaxies ever known. By assuming normal IMF, Holmberg IX is suggested to be born ~ 130 Myr ago from a bursty star formation event which then rapidly ceased, with very few stars formed in the past ~ 80 Myr that demarcates a lower age limit for the galactic mainbody; current star formation occurs only in outskirts, bringing a conundrum about the reason for the recent quenching in such a gas-rich environment. Contradicting general expectation for tidal dwarf galaxies hosting continuous star formation, the present quiescence implies Holmberg IX currently staying in a rarely-seen transient period. Without star formation continuing, Holmberg IX is likely transforming into a dwarf spheroidal galaxy, or oppositely into a(n) (ultra-)diffuse system which will probably dissolve in the end. Instead, if Holmberg IX possesses peculiar IMF and hosts low-mass, weak-Hα star formation, it is able to maintain long-term survival in its current status. On whichever evolutionary pathway in reality, Holmberg IX appears as a special case updating conventional understandings of tidal dwarf galaxies and hinting potential existence of similar analogs in the universe.

AI.tldr: Holmberg IX is a tidal dwarf galaxy with unusual properties: dust-poor despite gas-rich, globally young stars but deficient Hα emission, and current star formation only in outskirts, suggesting recent quenching in a gas-rich environment.
AI.motivation: To investigate the unique characteristics of tidal dwarf galaxy candidate Holmberg IX through multiwavelength analysis, challenging conventional understanding of tidal dwarf galaxies which typically host continuous star formation.
AI.method: Multi-wavelength investigation combining new deep Hα mapping observations with archival/published data to comprehensively probe dust, gas, and stellar populations in Holmberg IX.
AI.result: Holmberg IX shows: 1) dust dearth incompatible with its rich gas and metal content, 2) globally young stellar populations with prominent FUV but deficient Hα emissions, 3) formation ~130 Myr ago from bursty star formation that rapidly ceased, 4) very few stars formed in past ~80 Myr, 5) current star formation only in outskirts, creating a conundrum about recent quenching in gas-rich environment.
AI.conclusion: Holmberg IX represents a special case challenging conventional tidal dwarf galaxy models, currently in a rarely-seen transient period. It may evolve into a dwarf spheroidal or (ultra-)diffuse system, or survive long-term if it has peculiar IMF enabling low-mass, weak-Hα star formation. This discovery updates understanding of tidal dwarf galaxies and suggests potential existence of similar analogs in the universe.

title: A statistical study of the environmental age of core-collapse supernovae based on VLT/MUSE integral-field-unit spectroscopy
summary: We aim to understand the progenitor channels of CCSNe via a statistical study of the ages of their environments. We compiled a large and minimally biased sample of 129 CCSNe discovered by untargeted wide-field transient surveys and with archival VLT/MUSE integral-field-unit spectroscopy. We measured the local Hα luminosity within a 300-pc aperture centered on the SN explosion site as an empirical proxy for the environmental age. We find that the environments of Type II(P), IIb and Ib SNe do not show a significant age difference while Type Ic SNe are located in systematically younger environments than the other types (i.e. II $\approx$ IIb $\approx$ Ib > Ic). This is inconsistent with some previous reports of monotonically younger CCSNe environments with increasing envelope stripping (II > IIb > Ib > Ic). Our result suggests that Type Ic SNe have much younger and more massive progenitors than the other CCSN types and they likely originate from a distinct progenitor channel. The distinction between Types II(P), IIb and Ib SNe is insensitive to progenitor mass and mainly due to the different binary separation; in contrast, Type Ic SNe predominantly require much higher-mass progenitors accompanied by close companions with large mass ratios and/or much stronger stellar wind that depends sensitively on progenitor mass.

AI.tldr: Type Ic supernovae are found in systematically younger environments than other CCSN types, suggesting distinct progenitor channels with younger, more massive stars.
AI.motivation: To understand progenitor channels of core-collapse supernovae through statistical study of environmental ages, addressing inconsistencies in previous reports about age trends with envelope stripping.
AI.method: Compiled minimally biased sample of 129 CCSNe from untargeted surveys with VLT/MUSE spectroscopy; measured local Hα luminosity within 300-pc aperture as empirical proxy for environmental age.
AI.result: Type II(P), IIb and Ib SNe show no significant age differences (II ≈ IIb ≈ Ib), while Type Ic SNe are located in systematically younger environments (II ≈ IIb ≈ Ib > Ic).
AI.conclusion: Type Ic SNe have younger, more massive progenitors from distinct channels; II/IIb/Ib differences are mainly due to binary separation, while Ic SNe require high-mass progenitors with close companions, large mass ratios, and/or strong winds.

title: Tracing the relic nature of compact galaxies through their globular cluster systems
comment: 20 pages, 12 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We investigate the synthetic model of globular cluster (GC) systems of 17 compact massive galaxies (CMGs) from the Illustris TNG100 simulation to explore their connection with massive relic galaxies, systems that have undergone little structural evolution across cosmic time. The co-evolution of the GC systems and their host galaxies is based on a GC formation and evolution model that assigns clusters to stellar particles according to age and local conditions, providing positional, kinematic, and chemical information for individual GCs. By combining stellar assembly histories, effective radius evolution, and GC properties such as in-situ vs. ex-situ origin, metallicity, and spatial distribution, we identify consistent signatures of early formation and late-time accretion. We find that the GC mass fraction traces the host assembly history more robustly than the GC number fraction, as massive clusters better preserve the imprint of the early accretion history. Three CMGs from TNG100 emerge as strong massive relic analogs, exhibiting high in-situ GC fractions, narrow metallicity distributions, and compact spatial distributions. A tight correlation between the host stripped fraction and the extent of the ex-situ GC population further reveals the possibility to consider GC spatial profiles as a signature to identify tidal stripping processes. These results indicate that the combined analysis of GC populations and host stellar assembly offers a robust diagnostic for identifying massive relic galaxies and constraining their evolutionary histories.

AI.tldr: GC systems in compact massive galaxies trace assembly histories; GC mass fraction better tracks host evolution than number fraction; three galaxies identified as massive relic analogs with high in-situ GCs, narrow metallicity distributions, and compact spatial profiles.
AI.motivation: To explore the connection between globular cluster systems in compact massive galaxies and massive relic galaxies (systems with minimal structural evolution over cosmic time), using synthetic GC models to understand how GC properties trace galaxy assembly histories.
AI.method: Analyzed 17 compact massive galaxies from Illustris TNG100 simulation using a GC formation and evolution model that assigns clusters to stellar particles based on age and local conditions, providing positional, kinematic, and chemical information for individual GCs. Combined stellar assembly histories, effective radius evolution, and GC properties (in-situ vs. ex-situ origin, metallicity, spatial distribution).
AI.result: GC mass fraction traces host assembly history more robustly than GC number fraction; massive clusters better preserve early accretion history. Three CMGs identified as strong massive relic analogs with high in-situ GC fractions, narrow metallicity distributions, and compact spatial distributions. Found tight correlation between host stripped fraction and extent of ex-situ GC population, suggesting GC spatial profiles can identify tidal stripping processes.
AI.conclusion: Combined analysis of GC populations and host stellar assembly provides robust diagnostic for identifying massive relic galaxies and constraining their evolutionary histories, with GC spatial profiles potentially serving as signatures of tidal stripping processes.

title: Quantitative spectroscopy of single and multiple OB-type stars. Non-LTE spectrum analysis with machine learning
comment: 19 pages, 9 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: The plethora of spectra of OB-type stars in observatory archives and the much larger numbers to come from the WEAVE and 4MOST spectroscopic facilities require efficient, but also accurate and precise methods for (semi)automatic quantitative analyses. Neural networks were used to emulate the spectra of single- and multi-star systems, trained on hybrid non-local thermodynamic equilibrium (non-LTE) models that cover a wide range of atmospheric parameters and chemical compositions. To derive the full set of stellar atmospheric parameters and uncertainties, a Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithm was implemented to fit high-resolution spectra within 3000A-10500A. The neural networks and fitting algorithm were bundled into a programme called Spectral Analysis Tool Using Restricted Neural networks (SATURN). In its current implementation, SATURN facilitates the emulation of synthetic spectra for spectral types O7 to B9, which differ only negligibly from computed models. SATURN was tested on a number of benchmark stars that have been studied before, including single OB stars and a detached eclipsing binary (DEB) system. Excellent agreement of atmospheric parameters and elemental abundances for up to ten metal species is found with respect to the data in the literature, often with reduced uncertainties. For DEB components, the uncertainties are larger, in particular for the fainter secondaries when only a single-epoch spectrum is considered. Uncertainties of elemental abundances are typically <0.10dex. Some first applications of SATURN for analyses of new targets are shown to demonstrate its capabilities, such as fast rotators, including HD149757 (Zeta Ophiuchi). Consistent results are also found at reduced spectral resolutions relevant for observations with WEAVE and 4MOST.

AI.tldr: SATURN is a neural network-based spectral analysis tool for OB stars that emulates synthetic spectra and uses MCMC fitting to derive atmospheric parameters and abundances with high precision.
AI.motivation: The need for efficient, accurate, and precise methods for analyzing large volumes of OB-star spectra from current archives and upcoming facilities like WEAVE and 4MOST.
AI.method: Neural networks trained on hybrid non-LTE models emulate spectra of single/multi-star systems; MCMC algorithm fits high-resolution spectra (3000-10500Å) to derive atmospheric parameters and uncertainties.
AI.result: Excellent agreement with literature benchmarks for single OB stars (O7-B9), reduced uncertainties, <0.10dex abundance uncertainties; works for fast rotators and at reduced resolutions for WEAVE/4MOST.
AI.conclusion: SATURN provides accurate, precise, and efficient spectral analysis for OB stars, suitable for large-scale surveys and capable of handling challenging targets like fast rotators and binary systems.

title: Isochrone-cloud fitting and asteroseismology of the Kepler open cluster NGC6866
comment: Accepted for publication on A&A, 29/03/2026. Total 13 pages of main text with 13 figures, 3 pages of appendix with 5 figures
summary: We investigate how isochrones computed with different input physics and initial conditions affect the age dating of the open cluster NGC 6866, and compare the results with asteroseismic ages derived from Kepler photometry. Using Gaia DR3 data, we identified 180 cluster members with a clustering algorithm. We then developed an isochrone-cloud fitting method that accounts for a range of free parameters in the input physics. Variable stars were subsequently identified among the cluster members. For 19 g-mode pulsators, we carried out modelling with a dedicated grid of rotating stellar models, constrained by spectroscopic and photometric parameters, the asymptotic gravity-mode period spacing, and the near-core rotation rate. We considered two cases: modelling each pulsator individually and modelling them under the assumption of a common cluster age. PARSEC and MIST isochrones yield discrepant ages of 690 and 467 Myr, respectively. The isochrone-cloud fit indicates an initial critical rotation distribution peaking at 0.6, about a factor of two higher than inferred from asteroseismology. The seismic modelling shows agreement between seismic and isochronal masses, but substantial differences in the derived ages due to differences in internal mixing. When the g-mode pulsators are modelled with a shared cluster age, we obtain 759 Myr, consistent with the PARSEC isochronal age. We conclude that age dating of open clusters is sensitive to the adopted input physics and initial conditions, highlighting the need for better calibrated stellar evolutionary models.

AI.tldr: The study compares isochrone-based and asteroseismic age dating of open cluster NGC 6866, revealing significant discrepancies between different stellar evolution models and highlighting sensitivity to input physics and initial conditions.
AI.motivation: To investigate how different stellar evolution models (with varying input physics and initial conditions) affect age determination of open clusters, and to compare isochrone-based ages with asteroseismic ages from Kepler data for validation.
AI.method: 1) Identified 180 cluster members using Gaia DR3 data with clustering algorithms; 2) Developed isochrone-cloud fitting method accounting for range of free parameters; 3) Identified variable stars among members; 4) Modeled 19 g-mode pulsators with rotating stellar models constrained by spectroscopy, photometry, period spacing, and rotation rates; 5) Compared individual vs. shared cluster age modeling; 6) Used PARSEC and MIST isochrones for comparison.
AI.result: 1) PARSEC and MIST isochrones gave discrepant ages: 690 vs. 467 Myr; 2) Isochrone-cloud fit indicated initial critical rotation distribution peaking at 0.6, ~2× higher than asteroseismic inference; 3) Seismic modeling showed agreement in masses but substantial age differences due to internal mixing; 4) Shared cluster age modeling of g-mode pulsators yielded 759 Myr, consistent with PARSEC isochronal age.
AI.conclusion: Age dating of open clusters is highly sensitive to adopted input physics and initial conditions in stellar evolution models, highlighting the need for better calibrated models and demonstrating the value of asteroseismic constraints for validation.

title: DeepDISC-Euclid: Source Classification and Photometric Redshifts in Euclid Deep Field North With a Pixel-Level Deep Learning Approach
summary: The first Euclid Quick Data Release (Q1) provides extensive imaging and spectroscopic data for hundreds of millions of photometric objects across several deep fields. Accurate classifications and photometric redshifts (photo-z) for these sources are crucial to maximizing the value of these data. In this work, we perform source classification and photo-z estimation for the Euclid Deep Field North (EDF-N) around the North Ecliptic Pole, using a deep learning framework (DeepDISC) that learns and infers using 9-band images simultaneously. We train three dedicated models for (1) source detection and classification, (2) galaxy photo-z, and (3) quasar photo-z. The Euclid Q1 input source catalog, and classifications and spectroscopic redshifts (spec-z) from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument Data Release 1 are adopted as our training data. DeepDISC source detection achieves overall completeness of ~93% and purity of ~80% if using the Euclid source catalog as the ground truth. Using a JWST source catalog within EDF-N as the reference, we estimate a true purity of ~ 90% for DeepDISC sources. About 99.2%, 99.0%, and 84.8% of stars, galaxies, and quasars, respectively, are correctly recovered with their spectroscopic classifications. The DeepDISC photo-zs show good agreement with spectroscopic redshifts, for both galaxies and quasars. Comparisons with other Euclid Q1 products demonstrate that DeepDISC provides comparable or improved performance in source detection/deblending, classification and photo-z, especially for quasars. These results demonstrate the potential of pixel-level deep learning approaches for large-scale sky surveys such as Euclid and Roman, which will continue to improve with better training labels. We release the full DeepDISC source catalog (~13 million objects) for EDF-N with classifications and photo-zs, including photo-z probability distributions.

AI.tldr: DeepDISC deep learning framework performs source detection, classification, and photometric redshift estimation for Euclid Q1 data, achieving high completeness (~93%), purity (~90%), and accurate photo-zs comparable to spectroscopic redshifts.
AI.motivation: Accurate source classifications and photometric redshifts are crucial for maximizing the scientific value of the extensive Euclid Quick Data Release (Q1) imaging and spectroscopic data covering hundreds of millions of photometric objects across deep fields.
AI.method: Developed DeepDISC, a deep learning framework that learns and infers using 9-band images simultaneously. Trained three dedicated models: (1) source detection and classification, (2) galaxy photometric redshift estimation, and (3) quasar photometric redshift estimation. Used Euclid Q1 input source catalog and classifications/spectroscopic redshifts from DESI DR1 as training data.
AI.result: Source detection achieved ~93% completeness and ~80% purity (using Euclid catalog as ground truth), with estimated true purity of ~90% using JWST catalog. Classification accuracy: 99.2% for stars, 99.0% for galaxies, 84.8% for quasars. Photometric redshifts show good agreement with spectroscopic redshifts for both galaxies and quasars. Performance comparable or improved over other Euclid Q1 products, especially for quasars.
AI.conclusion: DeepDISC demonstrates the potential of pixel-level deep learning approaches for large-scale sky surveys like Euclid and Roman. The framework provides high-quality source catalogs with classifications and photometric redshifts, including probability distributions. Performance will continue to improve with better training labels. Full DeepDISC source catalog (~13 million objects) for EDF-N is released.

title: Parametric SED Modelling of Protoplanetary Discs: Validation and Application to an Unstudied YSO
summary: We present a physically motivated spectral energy distribution (SED) modelling framework for deriving stellar and circumstellar disc parameters from broadband photometry. The model combines a parametrized disc structure, dust opacity, and interstellar extinction within a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) inference scheme, allowing correlated parameters to be constrained self-consistently. Initial parameter estimates are obtained via non-linear least-squares fitting and subsequently refined through MCMC sampling. The method is first validated using the well-studied debris disc system 49 Cet, for which the model successfully reproduces key literature properties. It is then applied to the previously uncharacterised young stellar object (YSO) candidate 2MASS J02512618+6012576, using photometric measurements compiled from multiple surveys. The resulting fit indicates a late-type pre-main-sequence star surrounded by a substantial circumstellar disc consistent with a moderately embedded Class II object. We further assess the sensitivity of the inferred parameters to the adopted extinction law and find that the high reddening required by the model is robust against variations in $R_V$. This work demonstrates that physically meaningful constraints on disc structure can be obtained from broadband SED modelling when extinction and distance are treated within a statistically consistent framework.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian MCMC framework for deriving stellar and circumstellar disc parameters from broadband photometry, validated on debris disc 49 Cet and applied to YSO candidate 2MASS J02512618+6012576.
AI.motivation: To develop a physically motivated SED modelling framework that can derive meaningful constraints on disc structure from broadband photometry by self-consistently treating extinction and distance within a statistically consistent Bayesian inference scheme.
AI.method: Combines parametrized disc structure, dust opacity, and interstellar extinction within a Bayesian MCMC inference scheme. Initial parameter estimates from non-linear least-squares fitting, refined through MCMC sampling. Validated on debris disc system 49 Cet, then applied to YSO candidate 2MASS J02512618+6012576 using multi-survey photometry.
AI.result: Successfully reproduces key properties of 49 Cet. For 2MASS J02512618+6012576, identifies a late-type pre-main-sequence star with substantial circumstellar disc consistent with moderately embedded Class II object. High reddening requirement is robust against variations in extinction law parameter R_V.
AI.conclusion: Physically meaningful constraints on disc structure can be obtained from broadband SED modelling when extinction and distance are treated within a statistically consistent Bayesian framework, enabling characterization of previously unstudied YSOs.

title: Stars Born in the Wind II: Widespread Extra-planar Star Formation in M82's Halo
comment: 26 pages, 10 figures, 1 table; submitted for review to The Astrophysical Journal
summary: Galaxies evolve in tandem with their environments -- mergers and gas inflows drive galaxy growth while galactic outflows launched by supernovae may seed the galactic environment with gas, metals, and energy, fueling star-formation far from the main bodies of galaxies. The formation histories of young stars in the stellar halos of nearby galaxies can help understand this interplay. We thus present the most detailed map to date of young stars in the stellar halo of M82, a starburst galaxy in the M81 Group that hosts a prototypical outflow, using Hubble Space Telescope (HST) and Subaru Hyper-Suprime Cam observations. We find widespread extraplanar populations of stars with ages $\lesssim630$ Myr, with clear detections of stars up to $\sim5$ kpc to the south in unique arc-like stellar features (Southern Arcs) and in a new stellar trail up to $\sim20$ kpc to the east (M82's Tail), originating from the Southern Arcs. We estimate a total halo star formation of $\sim4\times10^6\,M_\odot$ in the last $630$ Myr. Overall, the star formation history (SFH) of the M82 Tail is correlated with periods of heightened star cluster formation in the M82 disk, which suggests the influence of the starburst outflow. Further, the fraction of young stars decreases as we move away from M82 to the east. We forward a picture where the M82 Tail formed from ram pressure stripped gas arising from M82's westward motion, triggered by shocks from the outflow.

AI.tldr: Detailed mapping reveals widespread young stars in M82's stellar halo, including arc-like features and a 20 kpc tail, suggesting formation from ram pressure stripped gas influenced by the starburst outflow.
AI.motivation: To understand the interplay between galaxy evolution and their environments, particularly how galactic outflows seed the environment with gas, metals, and energy that can fuel star formation far from the main galaxy body.
AI.method: Used Hubble Space Telescope (HST) and Subaru Hyper-Suprime Cam observations to create the most detailed map to date of young stars in the stellar halo of M82, a starburst galaxy with a prototypical outflow.
AI.result: Found widespread extraplanar populations of stars with ages ≲630 Myr, including unique arc-like stellar features (Southern Arcs) up to ∼5 kpc south and a new stellar trail (M82's Tail) extending ∼20 kpc east. Estimated total halo star formation of ∼4×10⁶ M⊙ in the last 630 Myr. The SFH of the M82 Tail correlates with periods of heightened star cluster formation in the M82 disk, and the fraction of young stars decreases with distance from M82 to the east.
AI.conclusion: The M82 Tail likely formed from ram pressure stripped gas arising from M82's westward motion, triggered by shocks from the outflow, demonstrating how starburst outflows can influence star formation in galactic halos and intergalactic environments.
